concepts.title: The UNIX Philosophy: Everything is a File

content.definition: In Linux and UNIX systems, 'everything is a file'. This means that physical hardware devices (like keyboards, hard drives, and printers), directories, and even running processes are exposed to user-space applications as standard file descriptors.
content.intuition: Imagine you write a program that outputs text to a file. In Linux, without changing your code, you can point that file descriptor to a network socket, a printer, or a graphical terminal. The program doesn't know the difference; it just writes bytes to a 'file'.
content.internals: This is achieved through the Virtual File System (VFS) layer. The kernel exposes standard operations: `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, and `close()`. Device drivers implement these functions for their specific hardware.
content.security_warning: Because devices are files, incorrect permissions on a device file (like `/dev/sda` which represents your main hard drive) can allow a regular user to read or overwrite the entire disk, bypassing all filesystem security.

mcqs.question: Which system call is universally used in Linux to send data to a hardware device, a file, or a network socket?
mcqs.explanation: Because 'everything is a file', the standard write() syscall is used to push bytes to any file descriptor, regardless of what underlying object it represents.

content.definition: Linux follows the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS), defining the directory structure and directory contents. The root of the filesystem is denoted by a forward slash `/`.
content.internals: Paths can be Absolute (starting from `/`) or Relative (starting from the current working directory). The special directories `.` represent the current directory, and `..` represent the parent directory.
content.linux_mapping: Key FHS locations: `/bin` (essential user binaries), `/etc` (host-specific system configuration), `/home` (user home directories), `/var` (variable data like logs), `/tmp` (temporary files deleted on reboot).

man_page.name: ls - list directory contents
man_page.description: List information about the FILEs (the current directory by default). Sort entries alphabetically if none of -cftuvSUX nor --sort is specified.

options.desc: do not ignore entries starting with .
use a long listing format showing permissions, owner, size, and timestamps
with -l and -s, print sizes like 1K 234M 2G etc.

commands.explanation: Change the shell working directory to /etc (absolute path).
Move up one directory level (relative path).
List all files (including hidden) in long format.

content.definition: Creating, modifying timestamps, and removing files/directories are fundamental operations. In Linux, removal is permanent; there is typically no 'Recycle Bin' at the command line level.
content.security_warning: The `rm -rf /` command will forcefully and recursively delete everything on the system without prompting for confirmation. It is catastrophic.

man_page.name: rm - remove files or directories
man_page.description: rm removes each specified file. By default, it does not remove directories.

options.desc: ignore nonexistent files and arguments, never prompt
remove directories and their contents recursively

commands.explanation: Creates a new directory named 'testdir' in the current working directory.
Creates an empty file named 'newfile.txt', or updates its access timestamp if it exists.
Recursively deletes 'testdir' and all its contents.
